speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Rachel Huber. The date is the 29th of May, 1996. My name is Vera Goldberg. Language of the interview will be English. And we are in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the interview with Rachel Huber. My name is Vera Goldberg. We are in Sydney, Australia. The date is the 28th of May, 1996. And the language of the interview will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape one of the interview with Rachel Huber. The date is the 28th of May, 1996. And we are in Sydney, Australia. Rachel, could you please give me your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel Huber.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name was Herscher-- H-E-R-S-C-H-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all Rachel Herscher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the place where I was born, my name was Rene. And my Jewish name was Rachel. But after the war, I decided I'm not French. I'm just Jewish. So taking the lesson of the, of the war in consideration, I became Rachel. And I, and I stayed Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where you got that name Rene from?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother must have, have, have read some beautiful book that she fell in love with, with the name Rene. I don't know. There must have been some French influence at that time in my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: My hometown was Chernivitsi with the interesting history. Until 1918, it belonged to the Austro-Hungarian empire. From 1918 until 1940, it was Romania. From 1940 till '41, it became Russia. It were communist Soviet Republic. 1941, we had a Romanian-German invasion, which is-- yes, I, I think that it-- after-- until the end of the war, it belonged to Romania. And then the Russians just took it back, as, as they usually do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you born in Chernivitsi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow up there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I grew up, until I left it for good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which part of Romania is Chernivitsi in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a province in the northern part of Romania. That was after the Ribbentrop--Molotov agreement, was handed over to, to the Russians. Two Chernivitsi basically got-- Bessarabia and Bukovina. And they both were heavily Jewish population. It had quite a amount of Jewish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Chernivitsi was in which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bukovina.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 16th of November, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what age does that make you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 70-- I'm 70 and a half already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit about Chernivitsi--

speaker: interviewee
text: Chernivitsi-- in, in, in the past, one usually sees only the, the pink side of a place. It was a lively Jewish city with a rich Jewish culture life. It was an exuberant city, as much as I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard for me to tell now, because these are one of the things that I don't recollect. I think that there were 100,000 or 120,000 Jews in, in, in Chernivitsi But I don't recollect it exact. That might have been less than that. But I don't recollect the exact number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there non-Jewish people who lived in Chernivitsi?

speaker: interviewee
text: The non-Jewish people that were in Chernivitsi were mostly Romanians that came with the administration after 1918, with the Romanian administration of the, of the city. But were a few professionals. There were peasants that were around the town. But in that town, I think there must have been maximum 10% Romanians, maximum that I recollect of.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the life there was a Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish life. It was a Jewish city with a very active Jewish life who had a lot of Zionist parties. There was the Bund. There was everything on the Jewish, on the Jewish interest there was. I was a bit too young to be so much part of it, as much as I know that my father was member of a Zionist organization. My grandfather belonged to one of the numerous [INAUDIBLE] temples, would you say here or shuls, synagogues, that we had in, in Chernivitsi. We had a Jewish theater. We had a Hebrew school. It was a nice city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an Orthodox Jewish town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is hard for me to, to answer that. Most people were traditionally religious. The-- all the variations that I've learned in the last few decades to, to [INAUDIBLE], I do not know how they were in Chernivitsi, It's something that I cannot answer. My grandfather was a traditional Orthodox Jew. My father not-- my father was in national thinking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jew. But he was not religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gustav Herscher. He was a tailor. He was born in Poland in a little town called Skala, which was on the Russian-Polish border. When he was in World War II-- World War I, sorry-- in World War I when he was a soldier in the Austrian army, he happened to be in Chernivitsi. And when the peace treaty was-- in Versailles was signed, he just remained in Chernivitsi. Because he liked the atmosphere. He liked the Jewish life there. And later on, he married my mother. And so he remained in Chernivitsi as a Polish citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a tailor by occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your father as a man.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a wonderful man, a really wonderful man. He was-- he was clever. He didn't have much education. He had only Jewish education, what going to cheder and going so on. But he was a voracious reader who educated himself all his life. He was warm. He was kind. He was the best father which a daughter could dream of. He was a wonderful grandfather to my two daughters. He was a good Jew. I think that the thing that I value so much for him was his consistency. When we were in the ghetto and he had a chance-- one of his former clients who was a priest, a Romanian priest, who approached him and suggested to him that if he would be baptized, he could give him a certificate that he is not Jew. And then eventually, he could maybe get out of the ghetto as a Christian. And his answer-- my father's answer was my father was a Jew. And my children-- my child is Jewish. And I'm Jewish. And if I have to die, then I'll die because I'm Jewish. But I can not change my religion. And I saw that under the circumstances that we lived then, which was a matter of life and death, he's standing upright and not being crushed by, by the surroundings and by the awful, hopeless situation in which we were. I thought showed me that he had colossal internal integrity. And if that was possible to love him more, I loved him even more for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Bertha. Schummer was her maiden name. My mother was a housewife. My mother was much more limited in her [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] horizon, I must say. She was a very good wife and a good mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She followed. She was a follower. She was not the leader type. She was a follower. Most probably, this was the traditional role of women at-- in that time. But if I look back, I sometimes wonder. [LAUGHS] Well.

speaker: interviewer
text: She-- where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Chernivitsi. But that-- certainly, she was born 1903. And that time, it was under Austrian Empire. And her-- she went German school, to Austrian School. And we spoke German at home because of all that. In Chernivitsi, basically most people talked German in spite that it was not a very pure German. Maybe it had 10% Yiddish in it-- but because of the influx of so many Polish Jews. But it still was German. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you speak Romanian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to school. I went to school. In this school, you have to speak Romanian. But I never used it outside the school. And that's one of the reasons most probably that I forgot so much from it. I had never used Romanian in the last 50 years or 55 years. So I could speak at that time, because I went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was an only child, unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about yourself as a little girl. What sort of little girl were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How can one be objective to oneself? That is impossible. What? Most probably a little stupid girl. I don't know, always trying to be a good girl. My father was a sickly man. He had all sorts of complaints. And my prime aim was always to be so good that my father would not get any pain. He would-- he had a heart condition or, or gallbladder. He was, he was sickly, as I said before.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think I had a certain amount of brightness. I was quite good in school. I, I was interested. I read a lot. What can I tell about myself? It's very hard. Where did you live in Chernivitsi?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [INAUDIBLE] Six was the name of the, of the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- it was a, it was a house that belonged to my grandfather. And it was a family house. My grandfather-- my grandmother lived there. And an auntie lived there with her family. And we lived there. It was a family house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of interaction with your uncles and aunts and cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes it was a traditional Jewish family from before the war. Every Shabbat, the family used to come together and enjoy socially one another. It was a-- it was a wonderful feeling to be part of a wider. Now everybody's so used to the nucleus family. Now that was a much wider, very supporting family. Whenever there were upsets-- and life dished us out quite a bit of upset-- one supported the other. It was a very warm surrounding in which our-- I cousins which were as if my brothers. It was a very warm, supportive atmosphere.

speaker: interviewer
text: The grandparents who owned the house were your paternal or maternal grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maternal grandparents-- I didn't know my paternal parents. My grandfather from father's side died when my father was two years old. And the mother was killed by the Germans during the war, as was my father's sister, sister-in-law, all, all, all family. The all family that they had in Poland was killed during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little about your grandparents that you knew, your mother's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was the, I think, the what we see were paterfamilias. He was the, the boss of the family. Well, each his word was law for the rest of the family. He was a traditional. It was Orthodox. My most beautiful memories I have from the [NON-ENGLISH] that we had, either Pesach or Rosh Hashanah, or, or Yom Kippur, it was wonderful to be part of all this ritual. All that-- those blessings that I used to get, I always was sure that when I am under my grandfather's prayer shawl and he blesses me that somebody upstairs, that God upstairs blesses me. He was a, a very imposing personality by his will and by his thoughts. It is funny when you ask me now, I sometimes think when I-- we sit on a Friday night and the family is all together and my grandchildren make [INAUDIBLE], I sometimes think that I wish my, my grandfather somewhere would see that his ways are not cut off, that we are going in his ways, and, and that I'm a good girl, that I'm bringing them up Jewish-- not only my children, but my grandchildren, too. And that would please him. Because it meant for him his religion and all that culture meant so much for him. That for us basically, as we say in Hebrew, [HEBREW]. That was his way of living and that he instilled of us-- and he always talked that when we grow up, that we have to go in the same path. And that was so instilled from childhood in me that I always feel that I have to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your granny?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother died early. I was, I think, five or six when she died. I remember her only as a very sick woman, very good, good-hearted, nice, always good to the grandchildren. All grannies are a dream to the grandchildren, but not much more about it that I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about going to school. Where did you start your schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started my schooling in Chernivitsi in a public school. School days are not a very pleasant memory for me. Because in school, we had to interact with non-Jewish Romanians who basically always showed their disdain and looked down to us because we were Jewish. And the staff, the teacher staff, was non-Jewish, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the primary school or the high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is primary school and later on in high school. I don't remember any particular incidents in primary school. Yet I remember quite a bit of my high school years, which were quite unpleasant in that respect, that in subjects that I was really excelled myself and was very good, I never got the, first the best mention or the best mark. Because she couldn't-- the teacher just wouldn't give a Jewish girl the same-- the best mark. I had several [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] happenings with my-- with the headmistress, who was a particular nasty person, who in an assembly once called me out for no reason whatsoever to look if I have-- if my eyebrows are not tinted. I was at that time maybe a girl of 12 or 13 who never would put-- I wore-- it was not-- at that, makeup was not used as here. But it was only part of a constant-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how would I say-- a constant putting down everyone that was Jewish. It was just showing their hatred, or that we are so much inferior that she has to take care the way we are combed to the way we are dressed or, or whatever. I had once a case-- it was my Romanian teacher from Romanian language came in. And that was before Easter. And she talked about Easter in literature, different Romanian authors that wrote about Easter. And then she said, and how do the Jews celebrate Easter, or something like that. And she says, you all know that the Jews use blood for baking their Easter bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then she called me out. And she says, you get up. And you tell them how you use the Christian blood. And I said, we don't use. And she started to, to mock me, you know? She brought me to tears at that time. And when I came home, I told my father the whole story. And I started to cry. And I said, why? And my father said, that's the-- that is what we have to pay for being Jewish. We have to accept that there is nothing we can do about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a certain-- I do not know the right word to use. But their mentality was already amongst Jews that you have to accept it. You have to take it, that it comes with existence. The persecution is part of, of Jewish existence. There was no hope at that time of a national homeland or whatever. Palestine at that time had very few Jews, in spite of there was already an awakening at that time. There were the so-called [HEBREW] that went to, to Israel. But there were very few numbers. Because in the '30s, there was already the time of the English mandate. And you had to be very, very wealthy to be able to afford the, the payment for a-- they called it certificate. That means a certificate that you are allowed to go to, to Israel. The English, but are they intent to keep the balance, that the Jews should not come in and the Arabs should come from all the parts-- from Jordan, from Syria, from [INAUDIBLE], from Egypt. They were very pro-Arab, as they always were. So there was no chance for us to go to Israel, to Palestine, as it always was called in that time. So the only thing that was left for us was to accept it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your family Zionistically inclined?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. My father-- my father very much wanted to go. But we didn't have the means.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to school a little bit. You mentioned that Chernivitsi had a very big Jewish population. What percentage of your class would have been Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard for me to, to, to tell you now after being out of school for, for 60 years, or 55 years, or whatever. I cannot tell you. Most probably, I will guess 70% were Jewish. But we didn't have strength in numbers because of our acceptance.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I look back now, I sort of think, how, how come that I took all that? How come that, that my father didn't go to school and didn't protest? Why did you do it to my, to my daughter or whatever? I can only-- if I try now to understand it, it's only-- I can only understand it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the all over passive attitude the Jews had at that time-- a colossal passivity. We are here to get the blows. And we have to take them. There was no rebellion, because there was no hope that you could change the situation there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that passivity and that overt anti-Semitism bear on life of the Jewish community in general? Were they taxed differently? Were there pogroms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't have any pogroms in Chernivitsi. We didn't have any. About tax, I must confess that I don't know. I was a girl. My father was complaining about taxes. But I cannot remember any-- there were discrimination, for instance, for a job, let's say, that we call now a public service. In public service, they were hardly would there be-- have been Jews in public service or the administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was-- there was sifting always. What are you? Are you Jewish? Then you are on the bottom of the list. There, there was colossal discrimination.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if there were laws in-- towards-- about discrimination, I cannot remember. I remember only that after the ascent of the Hitler and Hitler's men in, in Germany, the right wring in Romania started to assert itself. And in 1936, there was-- we call it the Iron Fist was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a Romanian called Zelea Codreanu came sort of the power. And then we had a-- on the town hall, we had a very big [NON-ENGLISH]. How you say it in English, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Swastika?

speaker: interviewee
text: Swastika, swastika, yes-- had a big swastika there. And there started to be-- to come out laws that we are not allowed to speak any other language than Romanian on the street, which made most people, most Jewish people in Chernivitsi, going [NON-ENGLISH], going-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't speak now English-- not talking. Because most people did not know Romanian. The women did not know. The men already knew a little bit to converse. But except the children, who knew Romanian in Chernivitsi-- very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: What dealings did your family have with non-Jewish people? You mentioned the story of the priest who was a friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father had clients. My father had non-Jewish clients that they came. He my father was self-employed. And he had several people working under him. And he had a very good clientele of non-Jews, too. They did not discriminate in that respect, the coming to Jew. Because they knew that, usually, Jewish plumbers or Jewish tailors or whatever was-- they knew their job. And they had to-- and it would be a good job. And they wouldn't be charged, overcharged. So this, this respect, my father had a lot of clients that were non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his relationship with, with the non-Jewish clients?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was quite, was quite OK. I don't remember any particular instances of complaint from my father's side. I don't remember that. I can't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about yourself? Did you have any social contact with non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very little, very little-- the others would not have liked to invite me to their place, to have a Jewish girl, Jewish daughter, a Jewish girl coming to their place for a visit. They wouldn't have like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you also never--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, I had all my Jewish friends. And they lived in the same area that I did. And that was enough for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how long did you spend at high school? How old were you when you started high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Soviets came in in June-- I think it was the 8th of June, 1940-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was, I think, 13 at that time. I think so-- can't remember-- 14. And I made-- I went one more year to school under the Soviets until the retreat. And then was World War II.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did, how did the school change under the Soviets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was completely different system. It was completely different system of teaching. First of all, that school under the Russians was a coeducational school, which were not used before. The whole atmosphere, the whole, the whole persecution that we felt as Jews stopped. Mind you, there were Russians, too, who would call you a [RUSSIAN] with their rolling R. [? Sarah ?] is a Jewish name. And they made it [RUSSIAN] in a, a, in a mocking way, in a-- they would, if they-- it was-- they were not allowed by law to make anti-Semitic remarks. But they wanted to show that they know who you are. And that would call on the street. [INAUDIBLE] [RUSSIAN]. That means he knows that I'm Jewish, so-- ah, but I didn't. I didn't mind. The year under the Russians was, was a very hard year from the acclimatization. We had to acclimatize ourselves to a complete new way of-- that was-- things were taught. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll continue with--
